Cobalt price quotations 1.1 London Metal Exchange (LME) Since February 22nd 2010, cobalt has been traded on the LME, making prices – at least for lower grade 99.3% but high volume metal – more transparent compared to freemarket quotations. The contract specifications are as follows:  Minimum 99.3%  Cathodes (broken or cut), ingots, briquettes or rounds  1 tonne lots (100-500kg drums of uniform size and weight)  Quoted in US dollars per tonne  Delivered by brand The LME announced that cobalt trade on the exchange amounted to 6,578 tonnes in 2011. CRU estimates that this volume was equivalent to 8.5% of world refined cobalt consumption in 2011. In the first half of 2012, LME cobalt trade totalled 5,592 tonnes. If cobalt trade increases in the second half of 2012 at the same pace as in the first half, we anticipate that full year cobalt trade on the LME will reach 11,184 tonnes, up by 70% from year-ago levels. That would equate to 13.7% of world refined cobalt consumption. This development would potentially support the role of the LME as an emerging marketplace for cobalt trade.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 75 CRU International Limited confidential 1.2 Price assessments Metal Bulletin (MB) and Platts Metals Week publish regular prices for cobalt. Prices are obtained from telephone surveys with traders and consumers in Europe and North America. Since 1991, Metal Bulletin has been publishing the prices of two grades of metal: 99.8% metal (high grade) and 99.3% metal (low grade). These are currently the de facto benchmark prices for cobalt, though Platts Metals Week assessments specifically for the US market are still prevalent there. Also in the USA, the US Defence Logistics Agency‟s (DLA) Strategic Materials Directorate had been selling cobalt from its stockpile since 1993, which at the time amounted to 52m pounds, with the sales price published after each transaction. DLA sales historically played a role in the price discovery process, but as stocks have diminished are now much less relevant, with only a handful of small transactions concluded each year depending on business conditions. In the 2009 financial year, for example, only 412,320 lbs were sold for just over $6.8m. No sales have taken place since 2009.

After 2000, several other price reporting systems emerged. The Cobalt Open Sales System (COSS) was an internet trading platform originally developed by WMC Resources for the sales of its 99.8% cobalt cathode, produced at Falconbridge‟s Nikkelverk refinery. BHP Billiton continued this system after the takeover of WMC. In addition to BHP Billiton, other producers, notably Norilsk and Jinchuan, provided quotations for their cobalt products via websites. Norilsk, in common with the BHP COSS site, also provided information on prices and volumes from recent transactions. As a further development of this type of trading system, Metal Bulletin introduced a Metal Price Watch system in 2004 where buyers and sellers are matched on an open auction system. These prices now form the basis of MB cobalt price quotations. However, two of these price information sources have now disappeared. In August 2008, Norilsk announced it would no longer publish a cobalt price, saying this was giving too much information away to rivals.

Furthermore, BHP decided in January 2009 to suspend the publication of the COSS price and the company now states it will direct market all of its cobalt production. In September 2007 Credit Suisse, in co-operation with commodity trader Glencore, launched an over-the-counter (OTC) futures contract for cobalt. This provides investors and other market participants with the opportunity to access the cobalt market in the absence of an exchange. Investors can buy a futures contract for a given month at a quoted price and the transaction is settled against the actual monthly average price quoted by Metal Bulletin. It is purely a cash- settled contract, but does give participants in the physical market the possibility of hedging price risk. Furthermore, Credit Suisse can source liquidity through an alliance with Glencore, which itself has the sales rights to a significant proportion of the world‟s cobalt through agreements with Xstrata and others. Other banks followed suit with Deutsche Bank and Macquarie both offering OTC contracts.

chunk.text: 76 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 CRU International Limited confidential 2. The history of cobalt prices 2.1 Prices prior to 1990 Before 1979, all cobalt metal was sold at the producer list price. Producers sold their cobalt in line with the Union Minière or, as it later became, the Gécamines (DRC) price. The so-called “freemarket price” began to emerge between 1978 and 1980, when a supply crisis loosened the producers‟ dominance of the pricing system. During the early 1980s, the free market broadly followed the producer price. In 1984, ZCCM and Gécamines made a bid to recover their price leadership position by restricting supplies in order to keep the price within agreed levels (US$11.45 to US$11.70/lb). At that time, supply and demand were more or less in balance. During 1985, the impact of capacity expansions resulted in production growing much faster than supply and, by the final quarter, stocks had grown to high levels. Furthermore, the freemarket was well supplied through barter deals and producers were selling openly to traders in order to move stocks. The result was a price fall from around US$11 to US$6/lb.

relations.evidence: all cobalt metal was sold at the producer list price

chunk.text: through barter deals and producers were selling openly to traders in order to move stocks. The result was a price fall from around US$11 to US$6/lb. At that time, Gécamines held a key position as the swing supplier. At times of high demand they could meet the additional requirements by selling from their stocks. Conversely, at times of weak demand, Gécamines would stockpile material while their competitors reduced prices to capture marginal market share and ensure they sold their full production. So, when Gécamines opted to sell more material to the market under these conditions in an attempt to capture market share, the price fell rapidly. As prices were still above production costs and cobalt was generally regarded as a by-product, there was little resistance to these falling prices. Only when Gécamines held prices fixed in 1986-87 did the price begin to stabilise. © 2012 CRU International Limited confidential 0 10 20 30 40 50 1986 1988 1990 1992 1994 1996 1998 2000 2002 2004 2006 2008 2010 2012 Nominal freemarket 99.8% price Real freemarket 99.8% price Freemarket prices have seen significant fluctuation US$/lb Data: MB

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 77 CRU International Limited confidential During 1987-90, Gécamines began to look at strategies to maximise its revenues from cobalt. They concluded that to do this in a sustainable way would require a steady increase in cobalt consumption over time. Furthermore, it concluded that this could only be achieved by ensuring stable pricing and secure supplies. Subsequently, the producer price was increased gradually in three iterative steps, with the free market price generally rising in anticipation of these announcements. Meanwhile, Gécamines also sought to establish long-term contracts with consumers in an attempt to persuade them that supplies would be secure and prices would be fixed. To encourage this, the company offered discounts of up to 10% on large tonnages. However, they failed to provide firm guarantees on price structure. As a result, many small and medium-sized consumers preferred to source their material from other producers and purchase at least part of their requirements from traders, in order to manage their exposure to a single supplier.

However by mid-way through 1990 Gécamines began to experience production difficulties. In June 1991, its Kamoto mine, which was an important source of cobalt-rich ore, suffered a serious collapse and production at this location fell to a third of previous levels. This was compounded by the rapidly deteriorating political situation in the DRC (then Zaire). Consumers with long-term contracts began to experience delays in delivery. The market became rife with uncertainty. Doubt was expressed in the media about Gécamines' ability to continue to supply cobalt in the long-term.

Against the backdrop of these supply disruptions the producer price became meaningless and, during 1991, the Zambians saw an opportunity to increase their prices by selling through traders at the freemarket price. Meanwhile, Gécamines was struggling to meet its supply contracts. As a result, all supplies of metal were being priced at freemarket prices, which reached US$30/lb during the early part of 1992, equivalent to a 4-fold increase in little over a year. At the end of 1991, the African producers followed the freemarket price, setting the producer price at US$25/lb. Meanwhile, there emerged an increased availability of supplies of Russian cobalt. Russian material was supplied in ingot form and at a lower grade of purity; 99.3% cobalt compared with 99.8% for Grade "A" cathode. Initially, many consumers were unwilling to take this material as they found the 3kg to 5kg ingots much more difficult to handle than granules or cut cathode.

The continued supply of material from the DRC and Zambia, coupled with increased availability of Russian cobalt, led to a sustained decline in cobalt prices. During June 1992, the freemarket price fell below the producer price and continued falling well into the third quarter of 1993. The African producers were uncertain how best to respond. Faced with a need to keep prices at US$25/lb in order to maintain revenue and the fear of a rapid decline in the metal price similar to that seen in 1985-86, they decided to maintain a higher producer price. The result was that Gécamines and ZCCM lost significant market share to the freemarket. Combined, stocks held by the two companies approached 9,000 tonnes by November 1992.

Both companies began to sell material, but there were frequent reports of sales at much lower prices to traders and large consumers as the free market price became dominant again. Subsequently, Zambia introduced a cobalt reference price of US$21/lb in consultation with Gécamines in April 1994, but its influence was negligible in the market. In February 1995, this reference price was raised to US$27.50. The main purpose of this reference price was to set a level at which the African producers could sell their metal committed on long-term contracts. However, since May 1996 this price has been well above the freemarket price and as supply constraints have been limited, this price quotation has been largely irrelevant. For this reason it is no longer quoted. Historically, cobalt prices have been characterised by strong volatility, with the peaks and troughs seen over the past 18 years correlating with certain key events.

chunk.text: balt prices have been characterised by strong volatility, with the peaks and troughs seen over the past 18 years correlating with certain key events. The peak of over US$30/lb in 1991 was caused by supply problems from DRC that followed the mine collapse at Kamoto that year and a serious civil disturbance in Katanga Province, where the Gécamines facilities are located. The subsequent trough (US$10/lb) in 1992 reflected a correction in purchasing patterns, partly as a result of high prices and partly because consumers had built up large inventories in the preceding months as they rushed to secure available supply. The next significant peak, in 1996, was the result of steadily increasing demand in a market where supply was tight. This was a period when trading activity was at its most influential. Further to that, prices dropped steadily as more supplies became available. The sharp downward spike in prices in January 1999 to US$7/lb was precipitated by sales of cobalt stock held by ZCCM to a leading trader. There were accusations of price manipulation over this transaction. However the price did not stay at that level for long and returned quite rapidly to over US$20/lb by mid-1999.

The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 79 CRU International Limited confidential 2.3 Price movements since 2007 2007 began against a backdrop of uncertainty over supply, with the immediate impact of the Norilsk announcement, Glencore‟s increasing influence over the market and concern over a lack of anticipated new supply coming on stream. These concerns were compounded by an explosion at the Chambishi furnace in Zambia in January, which deprived the market of around 150 tonnes per month, and ultimately resulted in a loss of 600 tonnes of supply. As a result, prices rose steadily in the first months of 2007, to break through US$30/lb during March, a 10- year high in nominal terms, at that time. A sharp reduction in imports of ores and concentrate from the DRC to China, amid speculation of a very tight supply situation, further contributed to the upward pressure on prices. The DRC government had already deemed the export of unprocessed ores illegal, in an effort to ensure value-adding processing occurred within the country. In addition, strong demand in the rechargeable battery and superalloy sectors ensured that market fundamentals remained tight.

chunk.text: 80 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 CRU International Limited confidential As 2007 drew to a close, the forecasts made by some market observers earlier in the year, notably Credit Suisse, that the price would reach a high of US$40/lb for the year were realised and the MB average at the turn of the year (99.8% cobalt) stood at around US$42/lb. Any hope amongst consumers that the market had peaked, at least in the short term, were confounded as prices continued to rise through the first three months of 2008. The year began with a series of brisk price increases as consumers feared an increasingly tight market with demand fundamentals strong and persistent uncertainty over security of supply. For example, reports in late January indicated that electricity blackouts in Zambia had led to reduced output in the country‟s copper belt, affecting operations including Chambishi and Mopani, and leading to fears that supply could not be met in the short term.

The first indications that the cobalt price would break through the landmark, and historic, US$50/lb level came in February when BHP Billiton raised its bid price on the website to US$51/lb following a series of sales close to that level. The price then peaked at a high of US$52.50/lb for 99.8% material, before falling back to around US$47/lb by the end of May. Prices then entered a period of decline, with a particular drop seen in August. However, this was followed by a sharp increase in September and prices remained relatively high throughout September and October. As November began, prices looked as if they had reached a stable level, though things quickly took a turn for the worse as the full impact of the banking crisis and erosion of economic confidence hit home. Demand started a downward spiral, worsening through Q4 2008 and persisting through Q1 2009. From a level of US$30-32/lb in early November 2008, 99.8% had fallen to as low as US$11.50/lb by early March 2009.

chunk.text: 84 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 CRU International Limited confidential Higher refined cobalt prices in China, compared to freemarket 99.3%, reflect the impact of high processing costs, as discussed in Chapter 2. As a result, high processing costs support refined cobalt prices in China; this is the reason why the two prices do not converge to each other. The processing component depends on operations-specific costs. However, as discussed earlier, the cost of raw materials is a major determinant, which depends on raw material patterns. The premium of Jinchuan electrolytic cobalt prices over freemarket 99.3% quotations averaged US$4.42/lb in January 2010 – July 2012. An important development in this context is the upstream integration of Chinese processors. As Chinese processors acquire stakes in mines, this enables them to have access to cobalt feed at considerably lower cost, as opposed to buying it from a third source. An example is Jinchuan‟s stake in Metorex. This enabled Jinchuan to incorporate the Ruashi mine in the DRC in its portfolio. chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 85 CRU International Limited confidential 4.2 Freemarket 99.3% vs. 99.8% quotations Most supply agreements are based on the 99.3% MB quotation, which is considered as the industry benchmark. The spread between the 99.3% and 99.8% quotations is referred to as the premium. The low (99.3%) and high (99.8%) grade markets are two different situations (connected, however), and therefore they each feature their own fundamentals. Hence, changes in the premium indicate market deficits or surpluses and sometimes trigger substitution effects between the two grades. In addition, different end-use sectors refer to different quotations. For instance, the low end of the 99.3% price is the benchmark for chemical production. The more technically demanding consumers, particularly in the superalloys industry have been responsible for increases in the premium. In the past, high premiums required for high grade cathode prompted consumers to substitute the lower grade Russian ingot in many applications that had previously used high grade material.

high grade cathode prompted consumers to substitute the lower grade Russian ingot in many applications that had previously used high grade material. However, during the course of the last ten years, consumers have become more adept at using different varieties of cobalt, thus enhancing their purchasing strategy through greater flexibility and increased bargaining power. The result has been a trend towards a decline in the premium. Since mid-2011, there has been an even faster decline in the premium. In particular since November 2011, the premium has been stabilised within the range of US$0.50-0.75/lb. It would be interesting to analyse how volatile the low and high end prices of each grade are. We conduct this in the period 4 January 2006 to 13 July 2012 by referring to the standard deviation of each series, which is a statistical measure indicating the degree of variation of a set of values around its average. We first examine the 99.8% price. In the above period, the low end averaged US$21.92/lb, with a standard deviation of 9.40. The high end had a mean of US$23.20/lb and a standard deviation of 9.57. We now turn to the 99.3% price.

Evaluating the magnitude of the standard deviation statistics, it appears that the least volatile of the above series is the low end of the 99.3% price, the series with the lowest standard deviation. On the other hand, the series with the highest variation around its average is the high end of the 99.8% price. The conclusions in terms of variability ranking are the same but the gain is that due to narrower price ranges, the results are easier to graph. The standard deviation statistic for freemarket prices was 1.81, while that of LME prices 1.79. As the widely accepted market benchmark, we would expect freemarket prices to reflect developments in the market in a better way. It is anticipated that due to the relatively small trade volume on the LME as a percentage of world demand (which we estimate at 8.5% in 2011), LME prices are more susceptible to extreme volatility that does not necessarily reflect the situation in the market. However, by conducting this analysis on a monthly basis, rather than using high frequency weekly data, we filter out such effects. CRU anticipates that as the LME cobalt trade volumes grow and the contract matures, the degree of convergence between LME and freemarket prices will rise. For fast adjustment between the two series, there are some important factors. Market liquidity is one of them, and this is expected to increase in the cobalt market. As we suggest in Chapter 1, cobalt consumption is projected to rise at a CAGR of 6.1% in 2000-2022. An expanding market size will bring more liquidity. Another factor is frequency of trading and price quotations.

88 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 CRU International Limited confidential When price data become more frequent, more arbitrage activity is projected to take place in the cobalt market. This has happened in other industrial metals, such as copper. In the case of copper, arbitrage activity between the LME, Comex, and SHFE tends to force prices to converge. When a price window opens, arbitrage activity causes price convergence. However, in the case of cobalt these developments may only happen in the long-run as they are also a function of how financialised a market is. In the foreseeable future, the cobalt market is not predicted to be as financialised as other metal markets are, such as aluminium and copper. From a fundamentals‟ perspective, even though LME cobalt trade has strong potential, there are certain limitations. As the LME is a principal-to-principal market, consumers have not direct access but have to buy metal through a member firm. Furthermore, there are supply limitations. The LME has regulations that producers need to comply with. Then, it is often in the producers‟ interest to sell quality material in the freemarket as they can realise a premium price.

chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 89 CRU International Limited confidential 5. The cobalt price outlook – medium-term 5.1 Introduction to price forecasting This chapter summarises the approach to forecasting the cobalt price adopted by CRU. We distinguish between the near/medium-term price forecast (2012-2017) and the long-term outlook for cobalt prices. The former is primarily based on the supply and demand fundamentals of the cobalt market, as discussed in Chapter 1 and 2. The long-term price outlook for cobalt follows CRU‟s development of the concept that long-run commodity prices are driven by the long-run marginal costs as well as the industry response to this cost of production by the behaviour of marginal producers and the creation of new capacity. 5.2 The near/medium-term price outlook methodology Over the past 20 years, the use of cobalt has moved from mainly metallurgical applications to a point where some 57% is currently used in chemical sectors. This has meant that refined cobalt is now produced and consumed in both metal and chemical forms.

relations.evidence: The use of cobalt has moved from mainly metallurgical applications to a point where some 57% is currently used in chemical sectors.

chunk.text: ere some 57% is currently used in chemical sectors. This has meant that refined cobalt is now produced and consumed in both metal and chemical forms. CRU believes that developments in cobalt prices are best understood when the fundamentals of both metal and chemical markets are taken into account. However, given the increasing degree of flexibility to switch from one grade to the other, distinguishing between the metallurgical and non-metallurgical sectors may not fully capture market developments. Indeed, deficits in one sector may be met by surpluses in the other, and that affects market prices. As a result, in the 2012 issue we take into consideration the market balance of the total refined market, which consists of both metallurgical and non-metallurgical sectors. This relationship comes from the findings of Chapter 1 and 2, which focused on refined demand and supply respectively. In addition to the refined market balance, we have also taken into consideration the balance at a raw material level. This consists of concentrate and intermediate products to supply the processing operations of cobalt metal and chemical producers who rely on purchased feed.

chunk.text: 90 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 CRU International Limited confidential 5.3 Low grade cobalt: market balance and price forecast CRU estimates that the world refined cobalt market balance exhibited a surplus of 2,781 tonnes in 2011. We believe that the surplus in 2012 will be considerably lower, amounting to 420 tonnes of cobalt. Refined consumption is expected to grow at a faster rate compared to production. Some small-sized Chinese processors are projected to cut production in 2012, impacted by low prices. Freemarket 99.3% prices averaged US$14.43/lb in H1 2012; we forecast that low grade prices will average US$14.00/lb in full year 2012. Even though the surplus is not predicted to be sizeable, we anticipate that high inventory levels at a concentrate and intermediate product level accumulated in 2009-2011 will weigh on refined prices. CRU expects that those inventories will meet the needs of a tight raw material market in 2011-2013, which is anticipated to see an accumulated deficit of 3,582 tonnes of contained cobalt. Looking ahead, we expect that refined production will grow strongly in 2013, generating a surplus of 3,171 tonnes.

3,582 tonnes of contained cobalt. Looking ahead, we expect that refined production will grow strongly in 2013, generating a surplus of 3,171 tonnes. In that year, we forecast that 99.3% prices will contract to US$13.00/lb. An even higher refined surplus of 4,173 tonnes predicted for 2014 will put more downward pressure on prices, which are expected to reach an average of US$12.80/lb. Despite the fact that a number of operations will not be profitable, they will stay in business in view of a forthcoming profitable period. The raw material market is projected to be balanced in 2014. A turning point is projected to take place in 2015, as consumption growth will outperform production, thus generating a lower surplus of 2,262 tonnes. We believe that prices will start rising, expected to average US$13.50/lb in 2015. An almost balanced refined market is anticipated for 2016, featuring a small surplus of 439 tonnes. CRU projects that prices will increase to US$15.00/lb. Turning to a refined material deficit of 1,549 tonnes, coupled with expectations of a tighter market, prices will be supported, reaching an average of US$17.00/lb in 2017.

The cobalt price outlook – long-term 6.1 The long-term price outlook methodology This section summarises CRU‟s approach to forecasting long-term cobalt prices. Essentially, it follows CRU‟s development of the concept that long-run commodity prices are driven by the long-run marginal cost of production (LRMC), as well as the industry response to this cost of production by the behaviour of marginal producers and the creation of new capacity. In the case of cobalt, developing this concept is more complicated than most other commodities and metals because it is produced in different forms and it is mainly a by-product of copper and nickel, which are more significant metals in terms of volume. 6.2 The long-term market balance Our supply and demand analysis suggests that there is going to be a deficit in the refined cobalt market in the long-run. This immediately implies that cobalt prices will increase in order to attract the development of new projects. With refined demand expected to grow at a CAGR of 6.1% in 2000-2022, sufficient supply will be needed to meet consumption needs.

relations.evidence: long-run commodity prices are driven by the long-run marginal cost of production (LRMC)

chunk.text: ent of new projects. With refined demand expected to grow at a CAGR of 6.1% in 2000-2022, sufficient supply will be needed to meet consumption needs. Our supply and demand balance shows that the refined market will exhibit a deficit of 28,840 tonnes in 2022. We project that the raw material market will be balanced in 2022 but this is only to meet the refined production forecast we present in Chapter 2, rather than world consumption needs. Within the cobalt feed supply analysis, we have assumed that there will be an increase in the supply of concentrates from the DRC in order to meet the cobalt feed needs, in particular those for Chinese processors. That may be affected by the DRC government restricting exports of concentrate in favour of intermediate products and capture domestically the value added. The fact that the raw material market appears to be balanced in 2022 does not mean that there will be enough cobalt units to meet refined demand needs. This is because the refined market will be in deficit, meaning that new processing capacity will be needed, especially in order to produce chemical products.

All values in this section are nominal, referring to 2022 US dollars. As suggested in our fundamentals analysis in the previous section, there is going to be a shortage of cobalt units in the long-run. Our new projects analysis in Chapter 2 shows that the outlook for new projects is uncertain, therefore it is not possible to assess which operations are most likely to add new capacity. Additional capacity can come from a variety of operations, featuring different locations, flow of raw materials and product forms. In addition, finance for those projects may come from different sources, hence affecting the capital charge of each project. As a result, the above factors may potentially generate a broad range of LRMC projections for the cobalt industry. Under these circumstances, defining categorically what the LRMC of the cobalt industry will be is not possible.

In the case of Chinese companies, the country risk premium is low, thus enabling them to have lower capital charges. The typical site cost and typical realisations cost originate from our 2017 cost analysis in Chapter 2, which have been adjusted for inflation. As we anticipated, non-integrated Chinese chemical producers who purchase feed are expected to exhibit the highest LRMC of production, forecast at US$23.58/lb in 2022. This is followed by the HPAL operation in the Pacific, the DRC carbonate project with refining by a third-party, and the blue chip company with extensive experience in high risk projects. The long-run cost of these projects is anticipated to range within US$8.21-9.76/lb. At the low end of the cost structure we have the DRC carbonate project with refinery in China, with a projected long-run cost of US$5.29/lb. It was anticipated that this kind of integrated operation would be the lowest cost project. Having predicted the above long-run costs from potential projects is not enough to forecast what the LRMC for the industry will be.

chunk.text: est cost project. Having predicted the above long-run costs from potential projects is not enough to forecast what the LRMC for the industry will be. Even though CRU believes that new capacity in the cobalt market will come in the form of the above projects, we need to include in our analysis the long- run cost profile of main product producers. We look at the examples of Chambishi, Idaho and CTT that can be found at the second part of Table 3.3, titled „Established operations expected to provide support to long-run prices.‟ Following the methodology explained above, we forecast that the long-run cost of these operations will be US$17.28/lb, US$15.36/lb, and US$11.57/lb respectively.

The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 95 CRU International Limited confidential As Table 3.3 illustrates, Chinese chemical processors that purchase concentrate are the marginal producers. However, when assessing costs in the long-run, a judgement call is to be made. We believe that the long-run cost level of US$23.58/lb is not a sustainable one, and raw material price negotiations are anticipated to take place. In addition, we believe that costs in the long-run will face downward pressure as a result of integration trends in the cobalt supply chain. Hence, CRU projects that the long-run cost of marginal producers will fall, the extent of which depends on the long-run costs of other projects in the industry. This is the reason why we included the main product producers in this long-run cost analysis, even though we anticipate that new capacity will come in the form of the first four types of projects in Table 3.3. After the high cost non-integrated Chinese processors, we believe that main product producers will provide support to the long-run cobalt price.

Considering the long-run cost analysis we conducted in Table 3.3, this is US$17.28/lb, which happens to be the long-run cost for Chambishi. Looking at the long-run cost structure of the cobalt industry, we believe that this is going to be the LRMC, expressed in 2022 nominal terms. This represents CRU‟s long-term cobalt price forecast. The above forecast is an indicative long-run price for cobalt, derived by the long-run costs of what we see as typical projects. The forecast does not incorporate the impact that the market balance, either surplus or deficit, will have on refined prices. Prices are expected to see fluctuations around the long-run level, depending on the conditions of the cobalt market. The cost analysis for 2022 does not follow the detailed methodology we have followed in our main cost analysis for 2012 and 2017 in Chapter 2. What we present here is an indicative cost structure that typical projects may face in 2022 in order to assess the addition of new capacity to the cobalt market.

CRU‟s projection for the long-run price of cobalt is not an outcome of supply and demand analysis as in the near/medium-term forecast. The price forecast generated for 2012-2017 was derived by an assessment of the expected status of the market balance in the above period. On the other hand, the long-run price projection is regarded as a long-run trigger price for marginal producers to add new capacity. Since the near/medium-term and long-run price forecasts are not the outcome of a single methodology, CRU does not graph them together. 6.4 Alternative scenarios in the price forecast As stated in earlier section in this chapter, our base case price forecast is based on the supply and demand fundamentals for the short/medium-term, and on LRMC for the long-term.

chunk.text: n this chapter, our base case price forecast is based on the supply and demand fundamentals for the short/medium-term, and on LRMC for the long-term. The underlying assumptions of the base case are explicitly discussed in Chapter 1 and 2. Our low case scenario is based on a weaker demand outlook and lower inflationary trends. In the short/medium-term, there is more downside rather than upside risk. A weaker price outlook in this case, is mainly affected by less robust demand. But even if the industrial outlook deteriorates rapidly, we still believe that demand for cobalt will be expanding, as analysed in Chapter 1. However, in an oversupplied market, prices could contract further, possibly to a low of US$11.52/lb in 2014. If the economic outlook is stronger than anticipated, we believe that the upside risk in the short/medium-term is limited, impacted by oversupply in the refined market.

chunk.text: 96 The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 CRU International Limited confidential Moving to the long-term, the situation in the cobalt market changes substantially. As we discussed in the supply and demand balance analysis, the cobalt market is expected to see deficits from 2017 onward. In a tight market, the upside risk is higher than the downside risk. To generate the long-term price forecast we conducted an indicative cost analysis and derived the LRMC of production. Since the long-term price forecast has not been generated by a long- term supply and demand analysis, applying the same high and low case scenarios as in the near/medium-forecast would not be correct. However, looking at the cost structure in Table 3.3, we understand that long-term prices cannot be lower than US$10.00/lb. In the absence of precise information on which projects will proceed, it is impossible to formally define a long- term price range. Overall, it depends on the performance of the global economy and in particular the cobalt-related end-use sectors. 6.5 Upside risks to the price outlook  Stronger than anticipated industrial and economic outlook.

in particular the cobalt-related end-use sectors. 6.5 Upside risks to the price outlook  Stronger than anticipated industrial and economic outlook. A stronger than expected industrial production activity will favour cobalt consumption. That will support several end- uses that consume superalloys. However, given the current economic outlook, the risks are mainly on the downside. A stronger than expected world economic activity will accelerate urbanisation trends, thus boosting the markets for high-end electronics and air travel.  Favourable end-use technology developments that require a higher cobalt intensity of use. chunk.text: The Outlook for Cobalt 2012 Chapter 3 97 CRU International Limited confidential  Delays in the start-up or ramp-up of new cobalt metal projects. New projects are always subject to operational and financial setbacks, even shortly before commissioning. Especially the projects located in higher risk regions may be subject to longer delays. Despite the fact that CRU has been conservative in our forecasts, delays are always possible.  Technical challenges at HPAL operations. There has been evidence that major HPAL operations are successfully commissioning. However, this kind of operations has historically experienced technical challenges that should not be disregarded when conducting a high and low case scenario analysis.  Financialisation of the cobalt market. This applies primarily in the long-run. As the cobalt market grows substantially in size, financial investors, in addition to the current physical players, may see this market as an alternative to more established metals markets. Investment will become easier as trade on the LME grows. However, this requires time. 6.6 Downside risks to the price outlook  Weaker than anticipated industrial production activity.

Considering the advanced level of downside macroeconomic risks, especially in industrialised countries, deterioration in industrial activity will have an impact on cobalt consumption. For instance, a weaker than expected world industrial activity will affect the market for cemented carbides as demand from the automotive, steel and shipbuilding industries will soften.  Unfavourable technology developments that trigger substitution of cobalt or lower intensity of use. Miniaturisation trends in end-use markets will affect cobalt consumption. This is very important in high-end devices that need to be lighter and smaller over time. The most critical end-use sector is lithium ion rechargeable batteries.  REACH regulations. A set of potentially tighter regulations may have an impact on cobalt consumption. Three REACH Consortia have been established by the Cobalt Development Institute (CDI) to implement REACH on behalf of the cobalt industry with the purpose of preparing the required registration dossiers for cobalt and cobalt compounds.

Our conservative forecasting approach applies both to large and small scale operations.  Aggressive development of new projects. If that takes place it will affect mainly the long- run outlook, considering the time needed to develop new projects. In view of the growing consumption volume of cobalt, as well as its contribution to high-end technologies, there has been an increasing interest from investment groups to be involved in the cobalt industry.
